name: DUTCH Test
aliases: Dried Urine Test for Comprehensive Hormones
short-definition: The DUTCH test (Dried Urine Test for Comprehensive Hormones) maps sex hormones, cortisol rhythm, and metabolites that standard blood and saliva testing miss.
full-definition:
What the DUTCH test measures

The DUTCH test — short for Dried Urine Test for Comprehensive Hormones — is a specialty hormone test performed on dried urine samples collected over 24 hours. Unlike a standard blood draw, which captures a single moment, DUTCH measures hormones and their metabolites across the day, giving a much more complete view of how the body produces and processes hormones.

A standard DUTCH panel covers:

  • Sex hormones: estrogen (E1, E2, E3), progesterone, testosterone, DHEA, and their metabolites
  • Cortisol rhythm: 4 timed cortisol readings plus cortisone — the full diurnal curve
  • Estrogen metabolism pathways: the 2-OH, 4-OH, and 16-OH pathways and how well your body methylates them
  • Androgen metabolites: including 5-alpha vs 5-beta pathways, which clarify Polycystic Ovary Syndrome and androgen excess
  • Organic acids (on the DUTCH Plus): markers of B vitamin status, neurotransmitter turnover, and oxidative stress

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, DUTCH is one of the most useful tools we have when sex hormones, cortisol, and detoxification all need to be evaluated together — particularly in perimenopause, Estrogen Dominance, unexplained fatigue, and persistent hormone symptoms despite normal blood labs.

Why it matters

Blood tests for estrogen and progesterone show only the 'parent' hormone at one moment. They cannot tell you:

  • Whether you are metabolizing estrogen down a protective or potentially carcinogenic pathway
  • Whether your cortisol rhythm rises and falls correctly through the day
  • Whether testosterone is being shunted into DHT (linked to acne and hair loss)
  • How well methylation is clearing used-up hormones

These pathways matter clinically. Two women with identical estradiol levels can have very different breast cancer risk, mood, and perimenopause symptom profiles depending on how they metabolize estrogen.

Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

DUTCH provides its own reference ranges based on sex, age, and menopausal status, with functional targets layered on top. There is no single 'normal' number — interpretation is pattern-based, looking at:

  • Estrogen metabolism balance (favoring the 2-OH pathway)
  • Adequate methylation of metabolites
  • A robust morning cortisol awakening response with a smooth decline
  • Appropriate progesterone-to-estrogen ratio for cycle phase or menopausal status

What abnormal results suggest

A DUTCH test can reveal estrogen dominance, poor estrogen detoxification, low progesterone, androgen excess driving acne or hair loss, blunted or reversed cortisol rhythm, and B-vitamin or methylation issues feeding into hormone problems. It is particularly useful when standard labs are 'normal' but symptoms are not — and when planning bioidentical hormone therapy, because the metabolite picture often guides which form (oral, transdermal, vaginal) is safest for an individual woman. Results should always be interpreted with a clinician trained in functional hormone testing.
common-questions:
DUTCH vs. blood vs. saliva — which is best?

Each has a place. Blood is best for thyroid, fasting metabolic markers, and a one-time snapshot of total hormone levels. Saliva is good for the cortisol curve and free (unbound) hormones. DUTCH offers the most comprehensive view — free hormones plus metabolites plus the cortisol curve in one panel — which is why we use it heavily in perimenopause and complex hormone cases. None of them replaces a thoughtful clinical history. The right test depends on the question being asked, which is why we choose testing case by case rather than running everything reflexively.

When in my cycle should I test?

If you are still cycling, DUTCH is best collected during the **luteal phase**, ideally days 19–22 of a 28-day cycle (or about a week before your expected period). This is when progesterone peaks and the estrogen-to-progesterone relationship is most clinically informative. If you are in perimenopause with irregular cycles, your clinician can help time the test or use a cycle-mapping version. In menopause or on continuous hormone therapy, timing matters less — collection can happen on any day.

Is the DUTCH test covered by insurance?

DUTCH is typically not covered by insurance and is paid out of pocket. Pricing varies by panel, with the DUTCH Complete and DUTCH Plus being the most commonly ordered. Many patients find the depth of information justifies the cost, especially when standard labs have not explained their symptoms or when planning hormone therapy. At MTC we only order DUTCH when we believe it will meaningfully change the treatment plan — not as a routine screen — so the cost translates into actionable answers.
seo-title: DUTCH Test: Comprehensive Hormone & Cortisol Testing | MTC
seo-meta-description: The DUTCH test maps hormones, metabolites, and cortisol rhythm in dried urine. Learn what it covers, when to use it, and how to interpret results.

name: Sex Hormone Binding Globulin
short-definition: SHBG (sex hormone binding globulin) is a liver protein that binds estrogen and testosterone, controlling how much free, active hormone reaches your tissues.
full-definition:
What SHBG measures

Sex hormone binding globulin — almost always abbreviated SHBG — is a protein made by the liver that grabs onto sex hormones (primarily testosterone and Estradiol) in the bloodstream. Hormones bound to SHBG are inactive; only the small fraction that remains free can enter cells and exert effects. The SHBG blood test measures how much of this binding protein is circulating, which directly determines how much free, active hormone is available.

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, SHBG is a critical lab in any workup of Polycystic Ovary Syndrome, Low Testosterone Women, unexplained acne or hair loss, perimenopause symptoms, and women who feel hormonally 'off' despite normal total hormone levels.

Why it matters

Total testosterone or total estradiol can look perfectly normal while SHBG is so high that very little hormone reaches the tissues — or so low that even normal hormone levels behave like an excess. SHBG is influenced by:

  • Insulin (high insulin lowers SHBG)
  • Thyroid hormone (hyperthyroidism raises SHBG, hypothyroidism lowers it)
  • Estrogen (estrogen, including oral birth control and oral hormone therapy, raises SHBG)
  • Liver health and inflammation
  • Body composition and protein intake

This makes SHBG a sensitive integrator of metabolic, thyroid, and hormonal health — one of the most informative single labs we run.

Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

Conventional reference range: roughly 20–155 nmol/L for adult women, though labs vary.

Functional/optimal target: typically 40–80 nmol/L for adult women, with adjustments for age and menopausal status. SHBG outside this window — even within the broad lab range — usually points to an underlying issue worth investigating.

SHBG should always be interpreted alongside total and free testosterone, estradiol, Fasting Insulin, and a full thyroid panel. A single SHBG number in isolation rarely tells the whole story.

What abnormal results suggest

Low SHBG (often <30 nmol/L) is most commonly driven by Insulin Resistance, obesity, hypothyroidism, or PCOS. Low SHBG raises free testosterone and free estrogen, contributing to acne, hirsutism, scalp hair loss, mood swings, and symptoms of estrogen dominance.

High SHBG (often >100 nmol/L) can be driven by oral estrogen (including birth control and oral hormone replacement), hyperthyroidism, undereating, very low body fat, liver disease, or genetic variation. High SHBG lowers free testosterone, contributing to low libido, fatigue, depression, and sluggish workouts even when total testosterone is normal.

The encouraging part: SHBG often shifts meaningfully when the upstream driver is treated. Resolving insulin resistance, optimizing thyroid hormone, switching from oral to transdermal estrogen, or addressing nutrition can move SHBG into a healthier range — and the symptoms typically follow. Work with a clinician who interprets SHBG in context rather than treating the number in isolation.
common-questions:
Why is my SHBG so high on birth control?

Oral estrogens — including most combined oral contraceptives — strongly stimulate the liver to produce SHBG, sometimes doubling or tripling baseline levels. The result is a sharp drop in free testosterone, which can produce low libido, fatigue, depression, and difficulty building muscle even years after stopping the pill in some women. SHBG often does come down after discontinuation, but recovery can take 6 months or longer. If you suspect this pattern, a full hormone evaluation including SHBG and free testosterone can clarify what is happening and guide next steps.

Can low SHBG cause weight gain?

Indirectly, yes. Low SHBG is a marker — not a cause — of the underlying problem, which is usually insulin resistance. Insulin resistance drives weight gain (especially abdominal), worsens PCOS, and lowers SHBG simultaneously. Treating the insulin resistance through nutrition, strength training, sleep, and sometimes medication tends to raise SHBG and support weight loss together. A low SHBG result is therefore valuable information: it points toward metabolic work that often resolves several symptoms at once.

Should I treat my SHBG directly?

SHBG is rarely treated as a target on its own. Instead, we treat the upstream drivers — insulin resistance, thyroid status, oral estrogen exposure, undereating, or liver health — and let SHBG normalize as a result. There are exceptions in advanced cases or when planning testosterone therapy in women, but those decisions belong with an experienced clinician. Chasing the SHBG number with supplements without understanding the cause usually produces disappointment and can mask the real issue.
seo-title: SHBG Test: What It Means for Hormones & Free Testosterone | MTC
seo-meta-description: SHBG controls how much free testosterone and estrogen reach your tissues. Learn the optimal range, what affects it, and what high or low results mean.

name: Estradiol (Lab)
short-definition: The estradiol lab (also called serum E2) measures the body's most active estrogen and is essential for evaluating perimenopause, menopause, and cycle health.
full-definition:
What the estradiol lab measures

The estradiol lab — also called serum E2 — is a blood test that measures circulating levels of Estradiol, the most biologically active form of estrogen. Estradiol is produced primarily by the ovaries during reproductive years and at lower levels by adipose tissue and the adrenal glands after menopause.

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, estradiol is a core lab in evaluating Perimenopause, Menopause, unexplained cycle changes, fertility concerns, and the symptoms that accompany hormone shifts — hot flashes, sleep disruption, brain fog, mood changes, vaginal dryness, and joint pain.

Why it matters

Estradiol affects nearly every system in the body — bone density, cardiovascular health, mood, cognition, skin, libido, vaginal tissue, and metabolism. As estradiol declines and fluctuates wildly through perimenopause, the symptoms that follow are often the first clue something is changing. A serum estradiol level, interpreted alongside Fsh, Lh, and clinical history, helps clarify where a woman is in her hormonal transition and whether hormone therapy is appropriate.

Estradiol does not work in isolation. It must be evaluated alongside Progesterone Lab, Shbg, thyroid status, and stress hormones for a meaningful picture.

Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

Estradiol varies dramatically with menstrual phase and life stage:

Conventional reference range (adult women):

  • Follicular phase (early cycle): 19–144 pg/mL
  • Mid-cycle peak: 64–357 pg/mL
  • Luteal phase: 56–214 pg/mL
  • Postmenopausal: typically <30 pg/mL
  • On hormone therapy: target varies by route and goal

Functional/optimal target: in cycling women, a robust ovulatory pattern with mid-cycle estradiol clearly higher than baseline. In menopause, when treating with hormone therapy, many clinicians aim for estradiol levels in the 50–100 pg/mL range to relieve symptoms and protect bone, though this is highly individualized.

A single estradiol reading without cycle context is hard to interpret in cycling women — that is why timing matters.

What abnormal results suggest

Low estradiol in a cycling woman can suggest hypothalamic amenorrhea (often from undereating, overtraining, or chronic stress), Premature Ovarian Insufficiency, pituitary dysfunction, or perimenopausal transition. In postmenopausal women, low estradiol is expected — but symptomatic women may benefit from carefully managed hormone therapy.

High estradiol can suggest ovarian cysts, certain ovarian tumors, perimenopausal estrogen surges, exogenous estrogen exposure, or — most commonly — relative progesterone deficiency producing functional Estrogen Dominance.

Estradiol is best interpreted by a clinician who looks at the full hormone picture, the menstrual cycle, and how a woman actually feels. The lab is one piece of the puzzle, not the whole answer.
common-questions:
When in my cycle should estradiol be drawn?

It depends on the question. To evaluate ovarian reserve and overall ovarian function, a day 3 (early follicular) draw paired with FSH is standard. To confirm ovulation or assess luteal function, a luteal-phase draw (around day 19–22 of a 28-day cycle) paired with progesterone is more useful. In perimenopause with irregular cycles, two or three draws across a cycle — or a DUTCH cycle map — give a fuller view than a single number. Always tell your clinician where you are in your cycle, because the same value means different things depending on the day.

Why is my estradiol high but I still have menopause symptoms?

Perimenopause is famously erratic. Estradiol can spike to higher-than-premenopausal levels one week and crash low the next, and symptoms often track with the change rather than the absolute number. A 'high' reading caught on a surge day is consistent with perimenopause, not with stable abundance. Symptoms also depend on the **ratio** of estrogen to progesterone — when progesterone falls faster than estrogen (a common perimenopausal pattern), women can feel symptomatic even with high estradiol on paper. Pattern recognition, not single labs, guides treatment.

Should I take estradiol if my level is low?

Not automatically. The decision to start estrogen therapy is based on your symptoms, your overall health, your personal and family medical history, and your goals — not on the lab number alone. Some women with technically low estradiol feel fine; others with mid-range estradiol feel terrible. Once therapy is started, estradiol levels are useful to confirm absorption (especially with patches and creams) and guide dosing. This is a conversation that belongs with a clinician trained in modern hormone replacement, who will weigh the full picture with you.
seo-title: Estradiol Lab Test: Normal Ranges & What It Means | MTC
seo-meta-description: The estradiol blood test (serum E2) evaluates ovarian function, perimenopause, and menopause. Learn the ranges by cycle phase and what results mean.

name: Progesterone (Lab)
short-definition: The progesterone lab (also called serum progesterone) confirms ovulation and evaluates luteal phase function, perimenopause, and estrogen-progesterone balance.
full-definition:
What the progesterone lab measures

The progesterone lab — also called serum progesterone — is a blood test that measures the level of progesterone, a hormone produced primarily by the corpus luteum after ovulation, by the placenta during pregnancy, and at smaller levels by the adrenal glands. Progesterone balances estrogen, supports the lining of the uterus, calms the nervous system, and protects sleep, mood, and bone.

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, progesterone is a key lab in evaluating Perimenopause, unexplained insomnia and anxiety, heavy or irregular periods, fertility concerns, and Estrogen Dominance.

Why it matters

Progesterone is the first ovarian hormone to decline as women approach perimenopause — typically 5 to 10 years before estrogen begins its meaningful drop. Because progesterone has calming, sleep-supportive, and anti-anxiety effects, its early decline often produces the very first perimenopausal symptoms women notice: 3 a.m. wake-ups, new anxiety, shorter or more variable cycles, and worsening PMS.

Progesterone also matters for:

  • Confirming ovulation — only an ovulating cycle produces meaningful progesterone
  • Protecting the uterine lining — opposing estrogen's proliferative effects
  • Supporting early pregnancy
  • Modulating GABA receptors in the brain, supporting sleep and calm

Without adequate progesterone, even normal estrogen levels can produce estrogen-dominant symptoms.

Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

Conventional reference range:

  • Follicular phase: <1 ng/mL
  • Luteal phase (peak): 5–20 ng/mL
  • Postmenopausal: <0.5 ng/mL
  • Pregnancy: rises significantly through trimesters

Functional/optimal target: a luteal-phase progesterone of at least 10 ng/mL is generally considered evidence of a robust ovulation. Levels of 15–20+ ng/mL are common in healthy young cycling women. Levels under 5 ng/mL during the expected luteal window suggest weak or absent ovulation.

What abnormal results suggest

Low progesterone in the luteal phase suggests anovulation or weak ovulation — common in PCOS, hypothyroidism, perimenopause, undereating, overtraining, and chronic stress. Symptoms include short cycles, spotting before periods, heavy bleeding, sleep disruption, anxiety, and difficulty conceiving.

Persistently elevated progesterone outside of pregnancy is unusual and may suggest a corpus luteum cyst, certain adrenal disorders, or laboratory error.

A single low or 'normal' progesterone reading without correct cycle timing is one of the most common sources of confusion in women's health labs. Progesterone results are only meaningful when timed correctly to the cycle. Interpretation in the context of symptoms, cycle length, and other hormones — including Estradiol Lab, Fsh, and thyroid markers — gives the clearest picture, and is best done with a clinician trained in functional hormone evaluation. Replacement, when appropriate, uses Progesterone Bioidentical formulations rather than synthetic progestins.
common-questions:
When should progesterone be tested?

If you have a regular cycle, the ideal day is 7 days before your expected period — usually day 21 of a 28-day cycle, day 24 of a 31-day cycle, and so on. This catches the luteal phase peak. Drawing too early or too late will undersell true progesterone production and may falsely suggest a problem that isn't there. If your cycles are irregular, your clinician may use multiple draws or pair the test with a [dutch-test] cycle map. Always note the first day of your last period when reporting the result.

Can low progesterone cause anxiety and insomnia?

Yes — and this is one of the most under-recognized patterns in perimenopause. Progesterone's metabolite, allopregnanolone, acts on GABA receptors in the brain, producing the same calming effect as anti-anxiety medications. When progesterone falls — whether from anovulation, perimenopause, or chronic stress — many women develop new anxiety, racing thoughts at night, and 3 a.m. wake-ups they cannot explain. Restoring ovulation (when possible) or carefully replacing progesterone often resolves these symptoms. This is an evaluation worth having if your sleep and mood have changed.

Is bioidentical progesterone safer than synthetic progestins?

Bioidentical progesterone — molecularly identical to what the body makes — has a different safety profile than synthetic progestins (medroxyprogesterone, norethindrone, levonorgestrel). Research suggests micronized progesterone has more favorable cardiovascular, breast, and mood profiles than synthetic progestins, with better sleep effects. That said, formulation, dose, route, and individual factors all matter, and progesterone is not appropriate for every situation. The decision belongs with a clinician trained in modern hormone therapy who can weigh your full history.
seo-title: Progesterone Lab Test: Optimal Range & Cycle Timing | MTC
seo-meta-description: The progesterone blood test confirms ovulation and evaluates perimenopause and luteal function. Learn timing, optimal range, and what low results mean.

When progesterone falls — whether from anovulation, perimenopause, or chronic stress — many women develop new anxiety, racing thoughts at night, and 3 a.m. wake-ups they cannot explain.       }

Research suggests micronized progesterone has more favorable cardiovascular, breast, and mood profiles than synthetic progestins, with better sleep effects. That said, formulation, dose, route, and individual factors all matter, and progesterone is not appropriate for every situation. The decision belongs with a clinician trained in modern hormone therapy who can weigh your full history."
      }
    }
  ]
}</script>

name: Testosterone (Total & Free)
short-definition: Total and free testosterone measure the body's primary androgen — essential for libido, energy, muscle, mood, and bone — in both reproductive and menopausal women.
full-definition:
What total and free testosterone measure

Total testosterone measures all testosterone in the bloodstream — both bound to carrier proteins like SHBG and the small fraction that is free. Free testosterone measures only the unbound, biologically active portion that can actually enter cells and exert effects. Both numbers are needed to understand androgen status accurately, especially in women, because Shbg strongly influences how much testosterone is actually doing the work.

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, testosterone testing is part of evaluating Low Testosterone Women, Polycystic Ovary Syndrome, unexplained low libido and fatigue, persistent acne or hair loss, and the perimenopausal and menopausal decline in androgens.

Why it matters

Testosterone is often labeled a 'male hormone,' but it is one of the most abundant biologically active hormones in women — produced by the ovaries, adrenal glands, and peripheral tissues. It supports:

  • Libido and sexual response
  • Energy, mood, and motivation
  • Muscle mass and strength
  • Bone density
  • Cognitive function and confidence

Testosterone declines gradually from a woman's twenties onward, and ovarian production drops sharply at menopause and after surgical ovary removal. Excess testosterone — common in PCOS — produces a very different clinical picture: acne, scalp hair loss, unwanted body hair, and irregular cycles.

Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

Conventional reference range (adult women):

  • Total testosterone: 8–60 ng/dL (varies by lab and age)
  • Free testosterone: 0.3–1.9 pg/mL

Functional/optimal target: for symptomatic women, many clinicians aim for total testosterone in the upper third of the female reference range and free testosterone in the upper half — provided there are no androgen-excess symptoms. There is no consensus 'optimal' number; the goal is the lowest dose that resolves symptoms safely.

Total testosterone alone can be misleading. A woman with high SHBG (often from oral birth control or hyperthyroidism) can have normal total testosterone but very low free testosterone — and feel every bit of the deficiency.

What abnormal results suggest

Low free testosterone is common in women on oral contraceptives, after oophorectomy, in chronic stress states, and naturally with aging — particularly through perimenopause and menopause. Symptoms include low libido, fatigue, low motivation, loss of muscle tone, and depression that does not respond to standard treatment.

High free testosterone is most often seen in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome, where insulin resistance drives ovarian androgen excess. It can also occur with adrenal hyperplasia, certain tumors (rare), or exogenous testosterone use. Symptoms include cystic acne, scalp thinning with frontal recession, unwanted facial or body hair, and irregular periods.

Testosterone is most accurately interpreted alongside SHBG, DHEA-S, Fasting Insulin, and full thyroid labs. Treatment — when indicated — should always be guided by a clinician trained in women's hormone therapy, because dosing and monitoring are very different from male testosterone protocols.
common-questions:
Why is my total testosterone normal but I still have symptoms?

This is one of the most common patterns we see. Total testosterone can sit in the middle of the lab range while free testosterone — the active fraction — is at the bottom or below it, usually because SHBG is high. Common causes of high SHBG include oral birth control, oral hormone replacement, hyperthyroidism, and undereating. Symptoms of low free testosterone (low libido, fatigue, low motivation, brain fog) can be just as severe in this pattern as in women with low total testosterone. The fix is to address the SHBG driver, not just look at the total number.

Should I take testosterone in menopause?

Testosterone therapy in women is supported by growing evidence for low libido (hypoactive sexual desire disorder) and may help with energy, mood, and muscle in selected women. It is not FDA-approved for women in the U.S., so prescriptions are off-label and require a knowledgeable clinician. Dosing is roughly one-tenth of male doses. With proper supervision, monitoring, and clear goals, testosterone can be a meaningful part of menopausal care for the right candidate. It is not a first-line therapy for everyone, and it is not appropriate without proper evaluation and follow-up.

Can high testosterone be lowered without medication?

Often yes, especially when the cause is PCOS or insulin resistance. The most effective lever is treating the underlying insulin resistance through nutrition, strength training, sleep, and sometimes medication like inositol or metformin. As insulin falls, SHBG rises, and free testosterone often comes down — taking acne, hair loss, and cycle irregularity with it. Stress reduction, adequate protein, and treating concurrent thyroid issues also help. Cosmetic anti-androgen medications are a separate question and can be discussed when lifestyle work isn't enough.
seo-title: Total & Free Testosterone in Women: Optimal Ranges | MTC
seo-meta-description: Total and free testosterone matter for libido, energy, muscle, and mood in women. Learn optimal ranges, why both numbers matter, and what results mean.

name: DHEA-S
short-definition: DHEA-S (DHEA sulfate, dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate) measures the most abundant adrenal hormone and reflects adrenal output, aging, and androgen reserve.
full-definition:
What DHEA-S measures

DHEA-S — short for DHEA sulfate or dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate — is a blood test that measures the sulfated, storage form of Dhea, the most abundant hormone produced by the adrenal glands. DHEA-S is the precursor from which the body can make both testosterone and estrogen, and its level reflects adrenal androgen production over a relatively stable window (the sulfated form is much steadier than DHEA itself).

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, DHEA-S is used to evaluate adrenal output, distinguish ovarian from adrenal causes of androgen excess in Polycystic Ovary Syndrome, explore unexplained fatigue, and assess whether women might benefit from carefully dosed DHEA replacement.

Why it matters

DHEA-S levels peak in the mid-20s and decline steadily with age — by menopause, levels are typically a fraction of what they were two decades earlier. This decline contributes to:

  • Reduced androgen reserve (libido, energy, muscle)
  • Less raw material for postmenopausal estrogen production
  • Lowered resilience to stress
  • Decreased bone density and skin elasticity

DHEA-S also responds to chronic stress: in some patterns, prolonged HPA axis dysregulation produces low DHEA-S alongside abnormal cortisol — a hallmark of long-term stress depletion. In other patterns (like classic PCOS), elevated DHEA-S contributes to androgen excess and acne.

Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

Conventional reference range (adult women): approximately 30–270 µg/dL, declining with age. Lab cutoffs vary, and most labs publish age-stratified ranges:

  • 20s: roughly 100–300 µg/dL
  • 30s: roughly 80–250 µg/dL
  • 40s: roughly 50–200 µg/dL
  • 50s and beyond: typically <150 µg/dL, often much lower

Functional/optimal target: for women, many clinicians aim for the upper half of the age-appropriate range, especially when symptoms of low androgen reserve are present. The goal is age-appropriate optimization, not chasing 20-year-old levels.

What abnormal results suggest

Low DHEA-S can suggest Adrenal Fatigue-pattern HPA axis dysregulation, advanced adrenal insufficiency (rare), pituitary disease, long-term steroid use, or simply normal age-related decline. Symptoms can include fatigue, low libido, low mood, poor stress tolerance, and reduced muscle mass.

High DHEA-S suggests adrenal androgen excess. Mild elevations are common in classic PCOS. Significant elevations may suggest non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia, an adrenal adenoma, or — rarely — an adrenal tumor, and warrant further imaging and evaluation. Symptoms include acne, hirsutism, scalp hair thinning, and oily skin.

DHEA-S is most useful when interpreted alongside total and free testosterone, Shbg, Cortisol Am, and a full thyroid panel. DHEA replacement is sometimes appropriate but requires careful clinical judgment — it converts to both testosterone and estrogen, so dosing must be individualized and monitored.
common-questions:
Should I take DHEA over the counter?

DHEA is sold as a supplement in the U.S., but that does not mean it is harmless or appropriate for everyone. Because DHEA converts to testosterone and estrogen, it can worsen acne, hair loss, and irregular cycles in women predisposed to androgen excess — and it is not appropriate during pregnancy or in many cancer histories. The right dose for a woman is typically much lower than what is sold on the shelf, often 5–25 mg rather than the 50–100 mg pills commonly available. We recommend testing first and only supplementing under clinical guidance with follow-up labs.

Why is my DHEA-S low if my cortisol is normal?

DHEA-S and cortisol are both made by the adrenal glands but follow somewhat independent regulation, especially under chronic stress. A common pattern in long-term HPA axis dysregulation is preserved cortisol (or even elevated) alongside low DHEA-S, reflecting the body's prioritization of stress hormones over restorative androgens. This 'cortisol-steal' or 'pregnenolone-steal' framework is more concept than precise mechanism, but the clinical pattern is real. Treating the underlying stress, sleep, nutrition, and thyroid issues usually allows DHEA-S to recover over months.

Does high DHEA-S always mean PCOS?

No. Elevated DHEA-S is common in PCOS but is not specific to it. Other causes include non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia, adrenal adenoma, certain medications, and — rarely — adrenal tumors. The pattern of androgen elevation matters: PCOS typically shows ovarian-pattern testosterone excess with mildly elevated DHEA-S, while adrenal-driven cases show more pronounced DHEA-S elevation. Significant elevations or sudden onset of androgen symptoms warrant further evaluation, including imaging when appropriate. A clinician familiar with these patterns can sort it out efficiently.
seo-title: DHEA-S Test: Adrenal Hormone Levels & What They Mean | MTC
seo-meta-description: DHEA-S reflects adrenal output and androgen reserve. Learn the optimal range by age, what high or low results suggest, and when DHEA replacement helps.

name: FSH
short-definition: FSH (follicle stimulating hormone) is a pituitary hormone that drives follicle development; rising FSH is a key marker of perimenopause and menopause.
full-definition:
What FSH measures

FSH — follicle stimulating hormone — is a hormone made by the pituitary gland that signals the ovaries to grow follicles each month and produce estrogen. The FSH blood test measures circulating FSH levels and is one of the central labs in evaluating menstrual cycle health, fertility, Perimenopause, and Menopause.

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, FSH is interpreted alongside Estradiol Lab, Lh, and Progesterone Lab to map where a woman is in her hormonal life and to clarify the cause of cycle changes, fertility concerns, or unexplained symptoms.

Why it matters

FSH operates on a feedback loop: when ovarian estrogen is low, the pituitary raises FSH to push the ovaries harder. As the ovaries age and become less responsive, FSH climbs higher to achieve the same response — and eventually rises persistently as menopause approaches. FSH is therefore a window into ovarian reserve and reproductive aging, but only when interpreted with proper cycle timing and the full hormone picture.

FSH also matters for:

  • Diagnosing Premature Ovarian Insufficiency (POI)
  • Distinguishing primary from secondary causes of amenorrhea
  • Evaluating fertility potential
  • Confirming menopause when symptoms are ambiguous

Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

Conventional reference range: varies dramatically by cycle phase and life stage:

  • Follicular phase (day 3 reference): typically 3.5–12.5 mIU/mL
  • Mid-cycle peak: higher transient surge
  • Luteal phase: 1.7–7.7 mIU/mL
  • Postmenopause: generally >25 mIU/mL, often 50–150+

Functional/optimal target: in cycling women, a day 3 FSH below 10 mIU/mL is generally reassuring for ovarian reserve. Day 3 FSH above 10–12 suggests diminishing reserve; above 25 suggests perimenopausal transition; persistently above 40 with low estradiol confirms menopause.

Like progesterone, FSH must be interpreted with proper cycle timing — a single 'random day' FSH in a cycling woman is often misleading.

What abnormal results suggest

Elevated FSH in a cycling woman suggests diminishing ovarian reserve, perimenopause, or — at very high levels with low estradiol — menopause. In younger women, persistently high FSH may indicate POI, an autoimmune ovarian process, or aftermath of certain medical treatments.

Low FSH in a woman not on hormonal contraception suggests hypothalamic or pituitary dysfunction. Common drivers include hypothalamic amenorrhea (from undereating, overtraining, severe stress), high prolactin, pituitary tumors, or medication effects. Low FSH in a woman with absent or irregular periods is a red flag worth investigating.

FSH alone rarely provides a diagnosis; it works as part of a panel. Hormonal contraception, recent hormone therapy, and timing all dramatically change interpretation. A clinician familiar with hormonal transitions will weigh FSH alongside estradiol, LH, prolactin, thyroid markers, and your symptoms to draw meaningful conclusions.
common-questions:
Can FSH alone diagnose menopause?

Not reliably on its own, especially in perimenopause. FSH can swing dramatically from week to week during the perimenopausal transition — a single elevated reading may or may not represent the true picture. The current standard is clinical: 12 consecutive months without a period, in the appropriate age range and absence of other causes. FSH supports the picture, particularly when paired with low estradiol, but is rarely required for diagnosis. Younger women with suspected premature ovarian insufficiency are an exception, where elevated FSH on two separate occasions is part of the diagnostic criteria.

Why is my FSH low if my periods stopped?

Low FSH with absent periods (functional hypothalamic amenorrhea pattern) is a different problem than menopause. It usually reflects the brain turning down ovarian signaling in response to stress: undereating, overtraining, severe psychological stress, or sometimes pituitary disease. The fix is rarely hormone therapy first — it is identifying and addressing the upstream stressor. Eating enough, resting, and reducing training load often restore the cycle within months. Persistent low FSH in this pattern warrants evaluation for prolactin, thyroid, and pituitary causes.

Does birth control affect FSH?

Yes, significantly. Combined hormonal contraception suppresses the entire hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, including FSH. While on the pill, FSH is artificially low and is not interpretable as a marker of ovarian reserve, perimenopause, or menopause. After stopping hormonal contraception, FSH and the rest of the cycle typically normalize over weeks to a few months in most women. If you want to evaluate ovarian reserve or transition status, plan to be off hormonal contraception for at least 2–3 months — and ideally have a natural cycle — before testing.

seo-meta-description: FSH evaluates ovarian function, perimenopause, and menopause. Learn the day 3 range, what high or low FSH means, and how to interpret results in context.

name: LH
short-definition: LH (luteinizing hormone) triggers ovulation each cycle and helps distinguish PCOS, hypothalamic amenorrhea, and menopausal patterns when paired with FSH.
full-definition:
What LH measures

LH — luteinizing hormone — is a pituitary hormone that, alongside Fsh, orchestrates the menstrual cycle. The mid-cycle LH surge is the trigger that releases the egg from the dominant follicle (ovulation). After ovulation, LH supports the corpus luteum's production of progesterone. The LH blood test measures circulating LH levels and is most informative when interpreted with cycle phase, FSH, and clinical context.

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, LH is part of evaluating cycle irregularity, suspected Polycystic Ovary Syndrome, confirming ovulation, and clarifying causes of amenorrhea (absent periods).

Why it matters

LH provides crucial information when looking at hormonal patterns:

  • The LH-to-FSH ratio helps distinguish PCOS (often LH:FSH >2:1) from hypothalamic causes of irregular cycles
  • An LH surge confirms imminent ovulation — the basis of ovulation predictor kits
  • Persistently elevated LH in older women is consistent with menopause
  • Persistently low LH suggests pituitary or hypothalamic dysfunction

LH does not work in isolation. The same number can mean very different things depending on cycle day, age, and the rest of the hormonal picture.

Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

Conventional reference range:

  • Follicular phase: roughly 2–10 mIU/mL
  • Mid-cycle surge: transient peak, often 25–80 mIU/mL
  • Luteal phase: 1–14 mIU/mL
  • Postmenopause: typically 14–60+ mIU/mL

Functional/optimal target: in cycling women, the early follicular LH should generally sit at or below FSH (LH:FSH ratio ≤1). A persistent ratio greater than 2 in the early follicular phase is one supportive marker of PCOS, especially when paired with elevated androgens and oligo-ovulation.

In perimenopause and menopause, LH rises persistently as the negative feedback from ovarian estrogen weakens. In hypothalamic amenorrhea, LH falls below normal — often disproportionately to FSH.

What abnormal results suggest

Elevated LH (with elevated LH:FSH ratio) in a younger cycling woman is often associated with PCOS, particularly when paired with insulin resistance, androgen excess, or oligo-ovulation. Persistently elevated LH at high absolute levels alongside low estradiol confirms menopause.

Low LH suggests hypothalamic or pituitary suppression — common causes include hypothalamic amenorrhea (undereating, overtraining, chronic stress), high prolactin, pituitary tumors, certain medications, and severe systemic illness.

LH is also affected by hormonal contraception (suppressed) and recent hormone therapy. Like FSH, LH should be interpreted only when cycle timing, contraceptive status, and the full hormone panel — including estradiol, FSH, Prolactin, thyroid markers, and androgens — are considered together. A clinician familiar with reproductive endocrinology will look at the pattern, not the single number.
common-questions:
What does an LH:FSH ratio greater than 2 mean?

An early follicular (day 3) LH:FSH ratio greater than 2 is often described as supportive of PCOS, particularly when other PCOS features are present (irregular cycles, androgen excess, polycystic ovaries on ultrasound, insulin resistance). However, the ratio is not diagnostic on its own — many women with PCOS have a normal ratio, and not all women with an elevated ratio have PCOS. PCOS is diagnosed clinically using established criteria. The ratio is one piece of supporting information, not a stand-alone test, and should be interpreted alongside the rest of the workup.

Can I use LH to confirm I'm ovulating?

Yes — that is exactly what at-home ovulation predictor kits detect. They measure urinary LH and turn positive when the LH surge begins, typically 24–36 hours before ovulation. A positive surge is highly suggestive of impending ovulation, but it is not absolute confirmation that ovulation occurred. The most reliable confirmation is a luteal-phase progesterone level (5–10 ng/mL or higher) drawn about a week after the surge. Combining ovulation kits with a luteal progesterone draw gives a much clearer picture than either alone.

Why is my LH low when my periods are absent?

Low LH with absent periods often points to hypothalamic amenorrhea: the brain has down-regulated ovarian signaling in response to a perceived stressor. The most common drivers in young women are undereating, overtraining, very low body fat, chronic psychological stress, and sometimes occult illness. High prolactin, pituitary disease, and severe systemic illness can also produce low LH. The treatment is rarely hormone replacement first — it is identifying and resolving the underlying signal that is suppressing the axis. Evaluation should include FSH, estradiol, prolactin, and thyroid labs to clarify the cause.
seo-title: LH Test: Luteinizing Hormone, Ovulation & PCOS Markers | MTC
seo-meta-description: LH triggers ovulation and helps identify PCOS, hypothalamic amenorrhea, and menopause. Learn cycle ranges, the LH:FSH ratio, and what results mean.

name: Prolactin
short-definition: Prolactin (PRL) is a pituitary hormone that supports lactation; elevated levels can disrupt cycles, fertility, and ovulation in non-pregnant, non-nursing women.
full-definition:
What prolactin measures

Prolactin — sometimes abbreviated PRL — is a hormone made by the pituitary gland that primarily supports breast development and milk production. The prolactin blood test measures circulating prolactin levels and is part of evaluating cycle irregularity, infertility, missed periods, unexplained breast discharge (galactorrhea), and certain headaches or vision changes.

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, prolactin is reviewed alongside Fsh, Lh, thyroid markers, and other reproductive hormones because elevated prolactin (hyperprolactinemia) is a frequently missed cause of cycle disruption and fertility struggles in otherwise healthy women.

Why it matters

Elevated prolactin suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, lowering FSH, LH, estradiol, and progesterone. This produces a recognizable cluster of symptoms:

  • Irregular or absent periods (Amenorrhea)
  • Difficulty conceiving
  • Decreased libido
  • Breast tenderness or unexpected milk discharge
  • Headaches or visual disturbances (when caused by a pituitary tumor large enough to affect surrounding structures)

Prolactin can also be elevated for benign reasons — recent breast stimulation, exercise, stress, certain medications, sleep, and even a stressful blood draw can transiently raise levels. Hypothyroidism is a particularly important and treatable cause: low thyroid hormone increases TRH from the hypothalamus, which stimulates prolactin alongside TSH.

Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

Conventional reference range: generally less than 25 ng/mL for non-pregnant adult women (lab-specific cutoffs vary slightly).

Functional/optimal target: typically less than 15–20 ng/mL in symptomatic women, with the goal being a level that does not interfere with cycle and ovarian function.

Prolactin should be drawn fasting, mid-morning, after avoiding breast stimulation and intense exercise that day. A single mildly elevated prolactin is often repeated under controlled conditions before further workup, because transient elevations are common.

What abnormal results suggest

Mild to moderate elevation (25–100 ng/mL): common causes include hypothyroidism, certain medications (antipsychotics, some antidepressants, opioids, dopamine antagonists), recent breast stimulation, and stress. Macroprolactinemia — large inactive prolactin complexes — can also produce elevated readings without symptoms and requires special lab analysis to identify.

Higher elevations (>100 ng/mL): more concerning for a Pituitary Adenoma (prolactinoma), the most common type of pituitary tumor. Levels above 200 ng/mL are highly suggestive of a prolactinoma and warrant pituitary MRI and endocrinology referral. Many prolactinomas are small, treatable with medication, and do not require surgery.

Low prolactin is rarely clinically significant.

Elevated prolactin is one of the most important treatable causes of cycle disruption in women — and one of the most commonly missed when only thyroid and basic hormones are checked. If your cycles are off, your fertility is unexplained, or you have unexplained breast discharge, prolactin belongs in the workup. The right next step depends on the level and the clinical picture, and is best worked through with an experienced clinician.
common-questions:
Can stress raise my prolactin?

Yes — both psychological stress and the physical stress of a blood draw can transiently elevate prolactin. This is one of the most common reasons for mild elevations in otherwise healthy women. Best practice is to retest after fasting, mid-morning, in a calm setting, having avoided breast stimulation, intense exercise, and large meals beforehand. If the repeat result is normal, no further workup is needed. If it remains elevated, the next step is to look for treatable causes — thyroid, medications, and pituitary imaging when appropriate.

Why does hypothyroidism raise prolactin?

When thyroid hormone is low, the hypothalamus releases more TRH (thyrotropin-releasing hormone) to push the pituitary to make more TSH. TRH also stimulates prolactin secretion as a side effect. The result is elevated prolactin alongside elevated TSH. Treating the underlying hypothyroidism almost always normalizes prolactin within weeks to a few months. This is one reason a basic thyroid panel — including [hashimotos-thyroiditis] antibodies when relevant — is part of any prolactin workup before chasing other causes.

Is a prolactinoma serious?

Most prolactinomas are small (microadenomas, less than 10 mm) and are managed with medication called dopamine agonists, which both shrink the tumor and lower prolactin. Cycles, fertility, and symptoms typically normalize on treatment. Larger tumors (macroadenomas) require closer monitoring, including pituitary MRI and sometimes ophthalmology referral if vision is affected. Surgery is reserved for select cases. With appropriate management, most women with prolactinomas live normal lives, conceive when desired, and have well-controlled symptoms long-term — which makes finding the diagnosis worthwhile.
seo-title: Prolactin Test: Hyperprolactinemia, Cycle & Fertility Issues | MTC
seo-meta-description: Prolactin can disrupt cycles, ovulation, and fertility when elevated. Learn the optimal range, common causes of high prolactin, and when to investigate further.

name: hs-CRP
short-definition: hs-CRP (high-sensitivity C-reactive protein) is a sensitive blood marker of systemic inflammation, used to assess cardiovascular and autoimmune risk.
full-definition:
What hs-CRP measures

hs-CRP — high-sensitivity C-reactive protein — is a blood test that measures small amounts of CRP, a protein produced by the liver in response to inflammation anywhere in the body. The 'high-sensitivity' version detects levels far below the threshold of standard CRP, making it useful for assessing chronic, low-grade inflammation rather than just acute infection.

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, hs-CRP is part of evaluating cardiovascular risk, Autoimmune Disease activity (including Hashimotos Thyroiditis), Metabolic Syndrome, and any clinical picture where systemic inflammation may be driving symptoms.

Why it matters

Chronic low-grade inflammation underlies many of the conditions women face in midlife — cardiovascular disease, autoimmune flares, metabolic dysfunction, depression, and cognitive decline. hs-CRP is a non-specific marker (it does not say where the inflammation is), but it is one of the most useful single screening tools because:

  • It tracks treatment response — falling hs-CRP often signals real progress
  • It identifies cardiovascular risk above and beyond cholesterol
  • It can flag autoimmune and metabolic activity before more specific markers move
  • It is inexpensive and widely available

hs-CRP fluctuates with acute illness, recent infection, surgery, intense exercise, and dental work. A high reading after the flu or a wisdom tooth extraction is not concerning — repeat testing 2–4 weeks later gives the more meaningful baseline.

Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

Conventional reference range:

  • Low cardiovascular risk: <1 mg/L
  • Average risk: 1–3 mg/L
  • High risk: >3 mg/L

Levels above 10 mg/L usually reflect an acute process (infection, injury, recent vaccination, autoimmune flare) and should be repeated after recovery.

Functional/optimal target: less than 1 mg/L, with many functional clinicians aiming for under 0.5 mg/L. Persistently elevated hs-CRP, even within the 'average risk' range, is worth investigating.

What abnormal results suggest

Elevated hs-CRP can reflect a wide range of causes:

  • Cardiovascular disease and atherosclerosis
  • Autoimmune conditions (including Hashimoto's, rheumatoid arthritis, lupus)
  • Insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome
  • Visceral obesity
  • Chronic infections (gum disease, sinus, urinary, gut)
  • Sleep apnea
  • Smoking, vaping, environmental exposures
  • Recent acute illness (transient)

Because hs-CRP is non-specific, the value is in interpreting it alongside the rest of the picture. A persistently elevated hs-CRP in a woman with thyroid antibodies, joint pain, and fatigue points one direction; the same number in a woman with metabolic syndrome and high blood pressure points another.

Low hs-CRP generally reflects low systemic inflammation and is reassuring.

The encouraging news: hs-CRP is highly responsive to lifestyle and root-cause work. Improving diet quality, treating insulin resistance, addressing sleep, resolving subclinical infections, and reducing alcohol all tend to lower hs-CRP within weeks to months. Tracking hs-CRP over time is one of the most motivating ways to see whether your underlying health is improving — and a conversation worth having with your clinician.
common-questions:
Should I worry about a single high hs-CRP?

Not necessarily. Acute illness, recent surgery, dental work, intense exercise, and even a heavy training week can transiently raise hs-CRP. A single elevated reading is rarely diagnostic. Best practice is to repeat the test 2–4 weeks later under more stable conditions, with no recent illness or major exertion. If it remains elevated, the next step is to look for the underlying driver — autoimmune, metabolic, infectious, or cardiovascular — rather than treating the number itself. Persistent elevation deserves investigation; a one-off bump usually does not.

Can hs-CRP track Hashimoto's activity?

Sometimes. hs-CRP is non-specific, so it captures inflammation broadly rather than thyroid-specific autoimmunity. In Hashimoto's, antibody levels (TPO and TgAb) and clinical symptoms are usually more direct measures of disease activity. That said, hs-CRP often falls when the broader autoimmune environment improves — when gut health, gluten exposure, nutrient status, and stress are addressed. Tracking hs-CRP alongside antibodies and symptoms gives a more complete picture of how the immune system is doing overall.

What lowers hs-CRP fastest?

The most consistently effective levers are: removing ultra-processed foods and seed oils, eating an anti-inflammatory whole-foods diet rich in omega-3s, treating insulin resistance, prioritizing 7–9 hours of sleep, treating sleep apnea if present, addressing periodontal disease, reducing alcohol, and treating underlying autoimmune or thyroid disease. Targeted nutrients — omega-3s, vitamin D, magnesium, curcumin in some cases — can support the process. Most women see meaningful drops within 8–12 weeks of consistent work, and the trend is more important than any single number.
seo-title: hs-CRP Test: Inflammation, Heart Risk & Autoimmune Marker | MTC
seo-meta-description: hs-CRP measures systemic inflammation linked to cardiovascular and autoimmune risk. Learn the optimal range, what affects results, and how to lower it.

name: Homocysteine
short-definition: Homocysteine (Hcy) is an amino acid that reflects methylation status and B-vitamin sufficiency; elevation raises cardiovascular, cognitive, and pregnancy risk.
full-definition:
What homocysteine measures

Homocysteine — abbreviated Hcy — is an amino acid produced as part of normal protein metabolism. Under healthy conditions, homocysteine is rapidly converted into other useful molecules using B vitamins (folate, B12, B6) and the methylation cycle. When this conversion is sluggish, homocysteine accumulates in the blood, where it damages blood vessels, contributes to oxidative stress, and impairs methylation-dependent processes throughout the body.

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, homocysteine is a useful marker of B-vitamin status, methylation function, cardiovascular risk, and broader metabolic health — particularly in women considering pregnancy, struggling with fatigue, or evaluating cognitive and mood symptoms.

Why it matters

Homocysteine sits at a crossroads of nutrient metabolism and integrates several systems at once:

  • Methylation status — the B-vitamin-dependent process that runs DNA expression, neurotransmitter synthesis, hormone clearance, and detoxification
  • Cardiovascular risk — elevated homocysteine damages endothelium and contributes to atherosclerosis
  • Cognitive health — high homocysteine is associated with accelerated brain atrophy and dementia risk
  • Pregnancy outcomes — elevated homocysteine raises risk of miscarriage, preeclampsia, and neural tube defects
  • Bone health — high levels are linked to fracture risk

Elevated homocysteine often signals insufficient bioavailable folate (Methylfolate), Vitamin B12, or B6, sometimes related to MTHFR variants, gut absorption issues, or medication effects.

Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

Conventional reference range: typically less than 15 µmol/L is labeled normal by most labs.

Functional/optimal target: less than 8 µmol/L, with many functional clinicians aiming for 6–7 µmol/L. Levels in the 9–14 range — within the 'normal' lab cutoff — are increasingly recognized as suboptimal, particularly for cardiovascular and brain health.

Homocysteine should be drawn fasting for the most accurate result.

What abnormal results suggest

Elevated homocysteine can reflect:

  • Inadequate folate, B12, or B6 intake or absorption
  • Genetic methylation variants (MTHFR C677T or A1298C)
  • Hypothyroidism (which slows methylation)
  • Renal insufficiency
  • Certain medications (metformin, methotrexate, anticonvulsants, proton pump inhibitors long-term)
  • Excessive alcohol or smoking
  • Older age

Mild to moderate elevation typically responds well to a methylation-supportive nutrient strategy: methylfolate, methyl-B12 (or hydroxy-B12), and adequate B6, alongside addressing any contributing conditions like hypothyroidism or kidney disease.

Low homocysteine is uncommon and rarely clinically significant on its own, though very low levels can occasionally reflect overmethylation patterns in some people.

Homocysteine is one of the most actionable lab markers in functional medicine — testing, addressing the cause, and retesting in 8–12 weeks usually shows meaningful improvement. If your number is creeping up, or you are planning pregnancy, this is a conversation worth having with a clinician trained in nutrient and methylation evaluation.
common-questions:
Do I need genetic testing if homocysteine is high?

Not necessarily. The most important question is whether the elevation responds to nutrient correction. Many people with normal MTHFR genetics still have elevated homocysteine from inadequate B-vitamin intake, gut absorption issues, hypothyroidism, or medications — and their levels normalize with appropriate support. MTHFR testing can help personalize the approach (favoring methylfolate over folic acid, for example) and is sometimes useful in pregnancy planning or recurrent miscarriage workups, but it is not required to begin treating elevated homocysteine. The lab value itself is the actionable signal.

Should I take folic acid or methylfolate?

For most women with elevated homocysteine — and especially anyone with known MTHFR variants — methylfolate (5-MTHF) is generally preferred over synthetic folic acid. Folic acid requires enzymatic conversion to be useful, and a meaningful fraction of the population has variants that slow that conversion. Methylfolate skips that step and is immediately bioavailable. Pairing it with methyl-B12 (or hydroxy-B12 in those who don't tolerate methyl forms) and adequate B6 typically lowers homocysteine within 8–12 weeks. Dosing should be guided by labs and a clinician, not guessed.

Why is homocysteine important in pregnancy?

Elevated homocysteine before and during pregnancy is associated with higher risk of miscarriage, preeclampsia, placental abruption, and neural tube defects in the baby. This is part of why prenatal vitamins emphasize folate so strongly. For women planning pregnancy or with a history of pregnancy complications, checking homocysteine before conception — and supplementing appropriately — is one of the most evidence-supported steps to optimize outcomes. This is especially relevant for women with MTHFR variants, autoimmune disease, or thyroid dysfunction, where homocysteine often runs higher.
seo-title: Homocysteine Test: Methylation, Heart & Brain Marker | MTC
seo-meta-description: Homocysteine reflects B-vitamin status and methylation. Learn the optimal range, what high levels mean, and how to lower it through nutrition and labs.

name: Comprehensive Metabolic Panel
short-definition: The comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP) is a 14-marker blood test covering kidney, liver, electrolytes, glucose, and protein status — a foundation of every workup.
full-definition:
What the comprehensive metabolic panel measures

The comprehensive metabolic panel — almost always abbreviated CMP — is a blood test that measures 14 markers covering electrolytes, kidney function, liver function, blood glucose, and protein status. It is one of the most commonly ordered tests in medicine and forms the foundation of nearly every initial workup.

A standard CMP includes:

  • Electrolytes: sodium, potassium, chloride, bicarbonate (CO2)
  • Kidney function: BUN, creatinine, eGFR
  • Glucose: Fasting Glucose
  • Calcium
  • Liver function: AST, ALT, alkaline phosphatase, total bilirubin
  • Protein status: total protein, albumin

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, the CMP is reviewed alongside thyroid labs, hormones, Lipid Panel, inflammation markers, and nutrient testing because metabolic, kidney, and liver function influence — and are influenced by — thyroid and hormone health.

Why it matters

The CMP is a broad screening tool that catches a wide range of conditions before symptoms appear — kidney dysfunction, liver disease, electrolyte imbalances, blood sugar problems, hydration status, and certain protein abnormalities. It also provides essential context for interpreting other labs:

  • Kidney function affects clearance of medications and supplements
  • Liver function influences hormone metabolism and detoxification
  • Albumin reflects nutrition and protein status, and binds many hormones and minerals
  • Electrolytes shift with adrenal function, dehydration, and certain hormonal conditions

A 'normal' CMP does not rule out subclinical disease, but trends within the normal range often tell more than a single value. Watching liver enzymes drift up over years, or eGFR drift down, can prompt action long before formal diagnosis.

Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

Each of the 14 markers has its own reference range. Highlights of common functional targets that differ from conventional ranges:

  • Fasting glucose: conventional 70–99 mg/dL; functional 70–90
  • ALT and AST: conventional often up to 40+; functional generally <25 (elevated values within range can suggest fatty liver)
  • eGFR: conventional >60 mL/min/1.73 m²; functional ideally >80–90 in younger adults
  • Albumin: conventional 3.5–5 g/dL; functional often 4.2+
  • BUN/creatinine ratio: conventional 10–20:1; ratios outside this can hint at hydration or protein status issues

Functional interpretation looks at the constellation of values, not single markers in isolation.

What abnormal results suggest

Deviations on the CMP point in many possible directions. Some examples:

  • Elevated liver enzymes: fatty liver, alcohol, medications, viral hepatitis, autoimmune hepatitis
  • Elevated BUN with normal creatinine: dehydration or high protein intake
  • Low albumin: poor nutrition, liver disease, kidney loss, chronic inflammation
  • Sodium or potassium abnormalities: medications, adrenal dysfunction, hydration, kidney issues
  • Elevated calcium: parathyroid disease, vitamin D excess, certain cancers

No single CMP result is diagnostic on its own. The pattern, the trend over time, and the clinical context are what guide next steps. A clinician familiar with both standard and functional interpretation will look at the whole panel together — and connect it to your thyroid, hormones, and symptoms — rather than treating each marker in isolation.
common-questions:
Do I need to fast for a CMP?

Yes — at least 8 hours of fasting is standard, primarily so the glucose result is interpretable. Non-fasting CMPs are sometimes ordered when only kidney or liver function is the question, but for a routine annual workup, fasting is the convention. Take only water during the fast (no coffee, tea, gum, or supplements), and schedule the draw in the morning. If you take medications, ask your clinician whether any should be paused, but do not stop prescribed medications without guidance.

What does it mean if my liver enzymes are slightly elevated?

Mildly elevated ALT or AST — within or just above the reference range — is one of the most common findings on a routine CMP. The leading cause in adults is non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), which is closely tied to insulin resistance and central weight gain. Other causes include alcohol, medications (statins, acetaminophen, certain antibiotics), supplements, viral hepatitis, and autoimmune hepatitis. Persistent elevation deserves a deeper look — abdominal ultrasound, hepatitis screening, and a focused metabolic workup typically clarify the picture.

How does thyroid disease affect the CMP?

Hypothyroidism can raise creatinine slightly, lower sodium in some cases, and modestly elevate liver enzymes. Hyperthyroidism can raise calcium and ALT. Severe thyroid disease in either direction can shift several markers at once. These changes are usually reversible with proper thyroid treatment, but they explain why a CMP alone — without thyroid labs — sometimes misses the underlying picture. At MTC we always interpret the CMP alongside thyroid status and clinical symptoms rather than in isolation.
seo-title: Comprehensive Metabolic Panel (CMP): Markers & Interpretation | MTC
seo-meta-description: The CMP measures kidney, liver, electrolyte, glucose, and protein status. Learn what each marker reveals and how to interpret results functionally.

name: Lipid Panel
short-definition: The lipid panel (cholesterol panel) measures total, LDL, HDL cholesterol, and triglycerides — core markers of cardiovascular and metabolic health.
full-definition:
What the lipid panel measures

The lipid panel — also called the cholesterol panel — is a blood test that measures the major fats circulating in the blood. A standard lipid panel includes:

  • Total cholesterol
  • LDL cholesterol ('low-density lipoprotein,' often called 'bad' cholesterol)
  • HDL cholesterol ('high-density lipoprotein,' often called 'good' cholesterol)
  • Triglycerides
  • Non-HDL cholesterol (calculated)

More advanced panels add apolipoprotein B (ApoB), lipoprotein(a) [Lp(a)], and LDL particle number for a more accurate cardiovascular risk picture.

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, the lipid panel is reviewed in the context of thyroid status, Fasting Insulin, Hs Crp, and overall metabolic health — because the lipid story is rarely just about saturated fat in the diet.

Why it matters

Lipids are essential. Cholesterol is a building block of every cell membrane, sex hormones, vitamin D, and bile. The problem is not the existence of cholesterol but the pattern and the underlying conditions driving it. Three patterns matter most:

  • High triglycerides + low HDL — the hallmark of insulin resistance and Metabolic Syndrome
  • Elevated ApoB and LDL particle number — the most accurate markers of atherosclerotic cardiovascular risk
  • Elevated Lp(a) — a largely genetic risk factor that should be checked at least once in every adult

Thyroid disease is one of the most under-recognized drivers of abnormal lipids. Hypothyroidism — even subclinical — can substantially raise LDL and total cholesterol. Many women have been started on cholesterol medication before anyone tested their thyroid properly, when treating the underlying Hypothyroidism would have been the appropriate first step.

Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

Conventional reference range:

  • Total cholesterol: <200 mg/dL desirable
  • LDL: <100 mg/dL optimal (lower if high cardiovascular risk)
  • HDL: >50 mg/dL for women
  • Triglycerides: <150 mg/dL
  • Non-HDL: <130 mg/dL

Functional/optimal target:

  • Triglycerides: <90 mg/dL (and ideally <80)
  • HDL: >60 mg/dL
  • Triglyceride:HDL ratio: <1.5 (a sensitive marker of insulin resistance)
  • ApoB: generally <80 mg/dL for primary prevention
  • Lp(a): <30 mg/dL (or <75 nmol/L)

What abnormal results suggest

High triglycerides with low HDL strongly suggests insulin resistance, metabolic syndrome, fatty liver, and visceral fat — far more than dietary fat. The fix is metabolic: reduce refined carbohydrates and ultra-processed foods, build muscle, prioritize sleep, and address Insulin Resistance.

Elevated LDL or ApoB raises cardiovascular risk and may reflect genetic factors, hypothyroidism, perimenopausal estrogen loss, dietary patterns, or Dyslipidemia. Treating the cause — including thyroid status — often improves the picture without medication. When risk is high, statins and other therapies have a role and should be discussed with your clinician.

Elevated Lp(a) is largely genetic, often missed, and meaningfully raises cardiovascular risk independent of LDL. It deserves checking at least once and informs the overall risk strategy.

Lipid results are best interpreted alongside metabolic, thyroid, and inflammation markers — not as a single number to react to. A clinician who treats root causes will look at the whole picture before recommending medication.
common-questions:
Why did my cholesterol go up in perimenopause?

Estrogen has favorable effects on lipids — it raises HDL, lowers LDL, and supports vascular health. As estrogen declines through perimenopause and menopause, LDL and total cholesterol commonly rise, and HDL often falls modestly. This is a normal physiologic shift, not necessarily a sign of new dietary failure. The right response is to look at the whole metabolic picture — thyroid status, insulin sensitivity, ApoB, Lp(a), inflammation — and address what is actionable. For some women, hormone therapy itself improves the lipid profile. The decision is individualized.

Is high LDL always bad?

LDL alone is an imperfect marker. Two people with the same LDL can have very different cardiovascular risk depending on particle number, particle size, ApoB, Lp(a), inflammation, and metabolic context. Small dense LDL particles (common with insulin resistance) are more atherogenic than large fluffy ones. ApoB measures the actual number of atherogenic particles directly and is increasingly preferred over LDL. We routinely add ApoB to the workup when cardiovascular risk assessment matters, rather than acting on LDL alone.

Should I test Lp(a)?

Yes — every adult should have Lp(a) checked at least once. It is largely genetic, does not change much with diet or exercise, and is a meaningful independent risk factor for heart attack and stroke. Roughly 1 in 5 people have elevated Lp(a) and most do not know it. Knowing your number changes the risk conversation: it raises the urgency of optimizing every other modifiable factor (LDL, ApoB, blood pressure, inflammation, lifestyle) and informs whether more aggressive treatment is warranted. It is one of the highest-yield single tests in cardiovascular prevention.
seo-title: Lipid Panel: Cholesterol, ApoB, Lp(a) & Optimal Levels | MTC
seo-meta-description: The lipid panel measures cholesterol and triglycerides for cardiovascular risk. Learn optimal ranges, why ApoB and Lp(a) matter, and how thyroid affects results.

name: Vitamin D (Lab)
short-definition: The vitamin D lab (25-hydroxy vitamin D, 25-OH D) measures the body's vitamin D stores — essential for thyroid, immune, bone, and hormone health.
full-definition:
What the vitamin D lab measures

The vitamin D lab — formally 25-hydroxy vitamin D, often abbreviated 25-OH D or 25(OH)D — is the standard blood test for assessing vitamin D status. It measures the storage form of the vitamin produced by the liver and reflects what your body has available from sunlight, food, and supplementation over the previous several weeks.

At Modern Thyroid Clinic, vitamin D is one of the most frequently abnormal labs we see — and one of the most actionable. It influences thyroid health, Autoimmune Disease activity, immune function, bone density, mood, and hormone signaling.

Why it matters

Vitamin D is technically a steroid hormone, with receptors on virtually every cell in the body. Adequate vitamin D supports:

  • Calcium absorption and bone mineralization
  • Immune system regulation, including reducing autoimmune flare risk
  • Thyroid hormone receptor function
  • Mood and cognitive health
  • Insulin sensitivity
  • Healthy pregnancy outcomes

Deficiency is widespread — particularly in women living at higher latitudes, those who spend most of their time indoors, women with darker skin, those with malabsorption conditions, and women over 50 whose skin synthesizes Vitamin D less efficiently. Low vitamin D is strongly associated with Hashimotos Thyroiditis activity and other autoimmune diseases, which is why it is part of every Hashimoto's workup at MTC.

Reference range vs. functional/optimal range

Conventional reference range: generally 30–100 ng/mL is considered 'sufficient,' with deficiency defined as <20 ng/mL and insufficiency as 20–29 ng/mL.

Functional/optimal target: typically 50–80 ng/mL, with many functional and integrative clinicians aiming for 60–70 ng/mL — particularly in women with autoimmune disease, thyroid disease, or osteopenia. Levels above 100 ng/mL are not necessary and very high levels (>150) can cause toxicity.

Vitamin D is best tested at least once a year, and more often during active correction of a deficiency. Levels fluctuate seasonally — typically lower at the end of winter and higher at the end of summer.

What abnormal results suggest

Low vitamin D is the most common finding. Causes include:

  • Limited sun exposure or sunscreen use
  • Higher latitude or seasonal change
  • Darker skin (more melanin, slower synthesis)
  • Age-related skin synthesis decline
  • Obesity (vitamin D is sequestered in adipose tissue)
  • Malabsorption (celiac, IBD, gastric bypass)
  • Chronic kidney or liver disease
  • Certain medications (anticonvulsants, glucocorticoids)

Symptoms of low vitamin D can include fatigue, frequent infections, low mood, muscle aches, bone pain, hair loss, and worsening autoimmune symptoms.

High vitamin D (>100 ng/mL) is almost always supplement-related. True toxicity (hypercalcemia, kidney stones, nausea) is rare but possible at very high levels (>150–200 ng/mL) sustained over time. Granulomatous diseases (sarcoidosis, certain lymphomas) can also raise vitamin D activation independently.

Vitamin D supplementation is generally safe and effective when guided by labs, but dose matters — and pairing vitamin D with vitamin K2 and adequate magnesium is part of a thoughtful protocol. Self-supplementing high doses without testing can occasionally cause problems. This is a conversation worth having with a clinician who tests, treats, and re-tests rather than guessing.
common-questions:
How much vitamin D should I take?

It depends on your starting level, body weight, sun exposure, absorption, and goals. A common starting strategy in adults with deficiency is 2,000–5,000 IU daily, with retesting in 8–12 weeks to confirm response. Some women need significantly more to reach the functional range, especially with autoimmune disease, obesity, or malabsorption. Others reach optimal levels on much less. Pairing vitamin D with vitamin K2 and adequate magnesium supports bone and cardiovascular outcomes. Dosing should be guided by labs and a clinician rather than a generic recommendation, because individual response varies meaningfully.

Why is my vitamin D still low after months of supplementing?

Several reasons are possible. Insufficient dose is the most common. Poor absorption — celiac, IBD, fatty liver, gastric bypass — is a frequent contributor. Obesity can sequester vitamin D in adipose tissue and lower serum levels even on adequate doses. Magnesium deficiency impairs vitamin D activation. Genetic variation in the vitamin D receptor and binding protein also affects how lab levels respond to a given dose. The fix is to look for the cause, increase the dose appropriately, and address contributing issues rather than simply pushing the supplement higher and higher.

Can vitamin D help my Hashimoto's?

Vitamin D plays an important role in immune regulation, and low vitamin D is consistently associated with higher Hashimoto's antibody levels and worse autoimmune control. Correcting deficiency is one of the most basic and evidence-supported steps in Hashimoto's care. Many women see meaningful improvement in fatigue, mood, and sometimes antibody levels when vitamin D is brought into the optimal range. It is not a stand-alone treatment — gut health, gluten exposure, stress, and other root causes still matter — but it is foundational, and rarely the place to skip.
seo-title: Vitamin D Lab Test (25-OH D): Optimal Levels & Meaning | MTC
seo-meta-description: The vitamin D blood test (25-OH D) measures vitamin D stores. Learn optimal ranges, what affects results, and why it matters for thyroid and autoimmune health.
